Given a set X ⊆ D that represents a safe subset of the
state space, the general objective of safe control design is
to find a control law π(x) that renders X forward invariant
under the closed-loop dynamics x˙ = f(x)+g(x)π(x), i.e., if
x(t0) ∈ X for some t0 ≥ 0, then x(t) ∈ X for all t ≥ t0. In
this paper, we assume that the safe set can be characterized
0

A. Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)

B. Neural Network-based CBF
The function c(·), which defines the boundary of the safe
set X , often fails to qualify as a CBF, either due to a lack of
differentiability or because it does not satisfy the invariance
condition (4). Thus, we must construct a CBF, either on a case-
by-case basis or using function approximation techniques.
Rn  R
Specifically,  let hθ(x) :  →  be the parameterized
function approximator. The parameters θ can be found
by solving the following self-supervise risk minimization
problem, in which the violation of both conditions (3) and
(4) is penalized:
2  2
minEx∼ρ[(−Iγ(hθ(x)))+ + λ(hθ(x) − c(x))  ],  (8)
+

Here ρ is typically a uniform distribution over D and
⊤

(9)
u∈U
This approach, however,  focuses solely on satisfying the
invariance conditions and does not  incorporate notions of
performance. Consequently,  it may  result  in an overly
conservative CBF in practice.

metadata.section_title: Main Idea: Domain Adaptation via Operator Learning

text:
The learned solution of problem (15) will be valid only
for a specific environment defined by the function c(·). As a
result, it cannot be deployed in novel environments without
retraining. This limits its generalization and usefulness in
real-time applications.
Instead of retraining a new CBF for each different envi-
ronment, we propose to learn the solution operator of the
0
PDE. Formally, given any c(·) ∈ C and the corresponding
1
solution h(·) ∈ C  that satisfies Nγ(c(x), h(x)) = 0 ∀x, we
0  1
define the solution operator G : C → C  as
h(x) = G(c(x)).  (16)
G(·)  is a nonlinear operator  that maps between infinite
dimensional Banach spaces. To approximately learn this
map, we assume that the input function c(·) is parameterized
Rne
by a finite-dimensional vector e ∈  that represents the
parameters of the environment, e.g., the location and sizes
of obstacles. Under this assumption, we can abstract the safe
set as X = {x | c(x, e) ≥ 0}.
By viewing the environment parameters as “virtual” states
without dynamics, i.e., e˙ = 0, we define the augmented state
as ξ = (x, e), obeying the following dynamics,
˙  aug  aug
ξ = f  (ξ) + g  (ξ)u,  (17)

In the next subsection, we provide the details of the proposed
training loss.
A. Training Loss Function
To train the CBF operator, hθ(x, e), we adopt a similar
procedure as [6] which for each environment approximates
the maximal control invariant set characterized by the HJ
PDE (14) and enhances the feasibility of the safety filter
with a CBF condition penalty term. We uniformly sample
M
the environmental parameters E = {ei}i=1 and the states
j N
Xe = {xe}j=1 for each environment. Then, we construct the
(cid:83)M
total dataset of joint states as Ξ =  i=1{ei} ×Xei , where
× denotes the Cartesian product of sets. We train the CBF
operator hθ(x, e) using two loss functions LHJ and LCBF.
LHJ for Maximal Control Invariant Set Approximation:
This loss guides the CBF hθ(·, e) to approximate the maximal
control invariant set for each environment

(20)

ξ∈Ξ
When the control input set U is polyhedral, the maximum
over u ∈ U in the PDE is achieved at one of the vertices and
can be obtained in closed form.

We evaluate the performance of the CBF operator learning
framework and demonstrate its effectiveness as an adaptive
safety filter through numerical experiments. In all experiments,
the MLP δθ consists of four hidden layers, each with 50

We proposed a deep operator learning framework to learn
domain-adaptive control barrier functions that can adapt to
novel environments without retraining. Leveraging domain
randomization, the proposed framework learns the solution
operator of a Hamilton-Jacobi PDE that maps the environment
parameters to a control barrier function that approximates
the maximal control invariant set. Our numerical experiments

show the effectiveness of the learned CBF to ensure safe
navigation in new environments. For future work, we will
incorporate a falsification/verification module to refine the
learned model based on counter-examples.
